content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

We do not just need larger context windows. We need memory that can connect code, documents, and decisions over time.
During one of my usual late-night research sessions , the kind where the day should be over, but your brain decides it is the perfect time to open twelve more tabs , I came across GraphifyLabs.ai.
It caught my attention.
Not because it looks like “yet another AI tool”, but because it seems to point toward a direction I find increasingly important: the shift from AI coding assistants that read files to AI coding assistants that build memory.
A quick disclaimer: I have not been able to properly test it yet. I am still on the whitelist, so this is not a hands-on review or a final judgment.
It is a reflection on a problem I keep noticing more and more: AI assistants have more and more context, but they do not always have a real memory of the project.
When we use Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, or similar tools, the first impression is almost magical. You ask the assistant to read a file, then a folder, then a function, then some documentation. And it does.
The problem is that this understanding is often tied to the session.
If you switch context, start a new chat, or the session becomes too heavy, part of that understanding has to be rebuilt. Not always from scratch, but almost.
And that is where the waste begins: tokens, time, attention, and continuity.
A project is not just a sum of files. It is a network of relationships: components, dependencies, decisions, documentation, bugs, workarounds, business logic, conventions, and choices made months earlier.
An AI coding assistant should not just read all of this.
It should remember it better.
Why Graphify caught my attention
From what Graphify communicates, it is trying to move exactly in this direction: turning code, documents, PDFs, Markdown, images, meetings, and other inputs into a queryable knowledge graph.
The claim is strong:
Any input. One graph. Complete recall.
It is clearly a marketing-driven promise, but the concept is interesting.
Instead of treating a project as a sequence of files to read one after another, Graphify tries to turn it into a relational memory.
Not just:
“Read this file.”
But:
“Which parts of the project are connected to this change?”
“Which previous decisions affect this feature?”
“Which documents explain this part of the codebase?”
“What actually changes if I touch this module?”
This is where the conversation becomes bigger than the tool itself.
Maybe larger context windows are not enough.
Maybe we need smarter memory.
1. Relationships between files, functions, and components
The first thing an AI coding assistant should remember better is technical relationships.
It is not enough to know that a file exists. The assistant needs to understand how that file lives inside the project.
Which components it uses.
Which modules it depends on.
Where it is imported.
Which functions it calls.
Which tests cover it.
Which parts could break if it changes.
This is one of the most delicate parts of a real codebase.
When we ask an assistant to change something, the risk is not only that it edits the wrong file. The risk is that it does not understand the effect of that change.
Maybe it changes a function, but misses the fact that it is used in three different places.
Maybe it fixes part of the frontend, but does not consider the API contract behind it.
A knowledge graph could be useful here: not to replace the model’s reasoning, but to give it a better map.
2. Decisions made over time
The second thing an AI coding assistant should remember better is decisions.
This is a big one.
In a project, many choices are not obvious from the code.
Why did we choose this library?
Why was this flow built this way?
Why did we avoid the apparently simpler solution?
Why was this feature postponed?
Often, the answer is not in the code.
It is in a call, a document, a note, a message, or a technical/commercial compromise.
AI assistants are getting better at reading the “what”, but they still struggle with the “why”.
And in real projects, the “why” matters a lot.
This is where the comparison with Obsidian becomes interesting.
Obsidian is a personal second brain. It helps connect ideas, notes, reflections, frameworks, and thoughts.
Graphify, at least from how it presents itself, seems to aim at something different: a computable memory of the project.
Obsidian helps human thinking.
Graphify could help the operational memory of AI agents.
I do not see them as alternatives.
I see them like this:
Obsidian to think.
Graphify to structure.
Claude Code or Cursor to execute.
3. Context that changes as the project evolves
The third thing an AI coding assistant should remember better is change.
Because a project is not static.
Files change.
Decisions change.
Documentation changes.
Priorities change.
Dependencies change.
Even if you build a good initial context, it can become outdated very quickly.
From what Graphify communicates, one of the most interesting parts is the idea of incremental graph updates: when a file changes, the whole graph does not need to be rebuilt. Only the affected part gets updated.
On paper, that is a meaningful difference.
Because in real projects, you do not want to rebuild everything every time.
You want the memory to stay aligned.
Imagine an advanced landing page for a client: code, copy, components, assets, positioning decisions, call notes, client requests, and a roadmap.
After two weeks, some initial decisions are no longer valid. A section has been removed. A CTA has changed. A message has been rewritten. A priority has shifted.
If the assistant keeps reasoning on old information, it risks producing the wrong output.
So memory should not only be persistent.
It should be updatable.
The use cases I find most interesting
Since I have not tested it yet, I prefer to talk about hypothetical but concrete use cases.
The first is refactoring on large codebases.
Instead of making the assistant search for dependencies every time, a graph could help it quickly understand which parts are connected.
The second is onboarding on existing projects.
When you enter an existing repo, you do not only need to read files. You need to understand history, critical nodes, dependencies, and previous decisions.
The third is living documentation.
Documents, meetings, notes, and code could become easier to query if they were connected in a relational way.
The fourth is consulting work.
For people entering client projects that are already in motion, a structured memory could reduce the time needed to understand what exists, what is still valid, and what is connected to what.
My current feeling
My feeling is that we are still too focused on one question:
“How much context can I give to the model?”
But maybe we should also start asking:
“What kind of memory am I building for the model?”
Because more context does not automatically mean more intelligence.
Sometimes it only means more noise.
I do not know yet if Graphify will deliver on all its promises. I will only understand that once I can test it properly.
But as a direction, I find it very interesting.
Because maybe the future of AI coding assistants will not be an infinite context window.
It will be better memory.
